items.question: Define E[X] and Var(X) for a discrete random variable. How are they related?
items.answer: E[X] = Σ x·p(x) is the probability-weighted average outcome. Var(X) = E[(X−E[X])²] = E[X²] − (E[X])² measures spread around the mean. Larger variance means more dispersion.

items.question: You have independent X and Y with E[X]=2, Var(X)=1, E[Y]=5, Var(Y)=4. What are E[3X−2Y+1] and Var(3X−2Y+1)?
items.answer: By linearity, E[3X−2Y+1] = 3·2 − 2·5 + 1 = −3. For independent variables, Var(3X−2Y+1) = 9·Var(X) + 4·Var(Y) = 9 + 16 = 25.

items.question: Why is MSE in regression connected to expectation and variance?
items.answer: For prediction ŷ, MSE = E[(Y−ŷ)²] decomposes into bias² and variance of the predictor plus irreducible noise Var(ε). Minimizing expected squared error targets the conditional mean E[Y|X] under squared loss.

items.question: A sample of n=100 has mean 12.3. Why is the sample mean itself a random variable before you collect data?
items.answer: X̄ = (1/n)ΣXᵢ depends on random draws Xᵢ. Before sampling, X̄ is uncertain with E[X̄]=μ and Var(X̄)=σ²/n. After observing data, you report one realized value 12.3 as an estimate of μ.
